attributes.stem:
A 24-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a five-year history of episodic breathing difficulty and cough. She reports that she primarily develops symptoms during and immediately following upper respiratory tract infections, though occasional episodes also occur during periods of stress at work. During flare periods she is functionally limited, often having recurrent coughing attacks while attempting to consult with clients at her job as a dietitian. Episodes are characterised by difficulty breathing in, severe dry coughing attacks, throat tightness, and a high-pitched noise during inspiration.

She was previously diagnosed with asthma clinically, but showed no improvement with inhaled or nebulised salbutamol. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There is no stridor at rest. Chest examination reveals normal air entry with no wheeze. Spirometry shows normal flow volume loops when asymptomatic, with no change with bronchodilators. Bronchial provocation testing is negative.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Speech pathology review and breathing exercises
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with features typical of inducible laryngeal obstruction (ILO), previously known as vocal cord dysfunction (VCD). The key features are episodic inspiratory symptoms such as throat tightness, coughing and inspiratory stridor that are triggered by specific factors such as viral infections, stress, cold air or exercise. The lack of response to bronchodilators, negative bronchial provocation testing, and the characteristically normal spirometry findings between flares support the diagnosis. ILO is frequently misdiagnosed as treatment-resistant asthma, leading to unnecessary escalation of asthma medications without clinical benefit.

Speech pathology review and breathing exercises is the first-line treatment for ILO. Speech pathologists can teach specific breathing techniques that help control laryngeal muscle function during episodes. These exercises focus on diaphragmatic breathing, relaxation techniques, and strategies to abort attacks when they occur.

Inhaled corticosteroids would not be effective as ILO is not an inflammatory condition like asthma. While some patients may have concurrent asthma, the described symptoms and negative bronchial provocation testing are typical of ILO rather than asthma.

Anxiolytic medication is not first-line treatment, though addressing underlying anxiety is important both for quality of life and trigger reduction. The initial approach to anxiety management should be psychotherapy, with pharmacotherapy considered as a second-line option when needed.

Botulinum toxin injection into the laryngeal muscles is reserved for severe, refractory cases of ILO that have not responded to conservative management. It would be inappropriate as an initial treatment.

Amitriptyline is sometimes used for chronic cough, but would not address the underlying mechanism of inappropriate laryngeal muscle contraction in ILO.

The key to managing ILO is early recognition and appropriate referral to speech pathology. Patient education about the condition and its triggers is also important. Some patients may benefit from psychological support if anxiety is a significant trigger, but this would be considered as an adjunct to breathing exercises.
learningPoints: Inducible laryngeal obstruction (ILO) is characterised by episodic and inappropriate laryngeal closure during inspiration.
Speech pathology intervention with breathing exercises is first-line treatment for Inducible laryngeal obstruction (ILO).
Inducible laryngeal obstruction (ILO) is commonly misdiagnosed as treatment-resistant asthma but can be distinguished by inspiratory stridor and lack of response to bronchodilators.
references: Røksund OD, Heimdal JH, Clemm H, Vollsæter M, Halvorsen T. Exercise inducible laryngeal obstruction: diagnostics and management. Paediatr Respir Rev. 2017 Jan;21:86-94. Available at: https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/27492717/

Sandage Mary J, Milstein Claudio F, Nauman E. Inducible Laryngeal Obstruction Differential Diagnosis in Adolescents and Adults: A Tutorial. American Journal of Speech-Language Pathology. 2023;32(1):1-17. Available at: https://pubs.asha.org/doi/abs/10.1044/2022_AJSLP-22-00187

Karlsen T, Vreim K, Røksund OD, Vollsæter M, Muralitharan P, Ellingsen TA, Heimdal JH, Halvorsen T, Clemm H. A speech therapy treatment protocol for exercise induced laryngeal obstruction. Front Pediatr. 2024 Jun 13;12:1356476. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC11208626/
